industries.name: Fundamental Nuclear & Atomic Research
uses: Named after Darmstadt, Germany — home of GSI. Produced by bombarding lead-208 with nickel-62 ions. Predicted to be a heavier platinum analog (Group 10) but relativistic effects significantly alter its electronic structure. The 6d transition metals (Rf through Cn) represent a frontier where relativistic quantum chemistry makes predictions that challenge classical periodic table expectations.
Longest-lived known isotope: 281Ds, t½ = 11 seconds. First synthesized: 1994, GSI Darmstadt (Hofmann group).

Research probes the limits of nuclear stability, tests relativistic quantum chemistry predictions in superheavy atoms, and searches for the theoretical island of stability predicted around Z=114, N=184.
All production requires specialized particle accelerators and isotope reactor facilities — this element cannot be made by any other means.

companies.name: GSI Helmholtz Centre
companies.use: Discovered darmstadtium in 1994 using the SHIP separator and lead-208 + nickel-62 cold fusion. Hofmann's group at GSI discovered elements 107–112 in a remarkable sequence spanning 1981–1996, establishing GSI as the world's premier cold fusion superheavy element laboratory.

companies.name: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
companies.use: Independently confirmed darmstadtium production and decay properties using the BGS (Berkeley Gas-filled Separator). LBNL's confirmation experiments provided the independent verification required by IUPAC for official element recognition.

companies.name: RIKEN Nishina Center
companies.use: Produced darmstadtium using the GARIS separator for nuclear spectroscopy studies of Ds isotopes. RIKEN's high-intensity heavy-ion beams enabled detailed gamma-ray and conversion electron spectroscopy of darmstadtium decay chains.
